narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 49% chance of not passing. Picture the model as an experienced advisor who's reviewed thousands of stories like yours, and it zeroed in first on your LSAT score of 22.5, which stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward potential failure, like a weak opening chapter that sets a cautious tone for the whole book. Then came your Year 3 decile of 1.0, reinforcing that early struggles in law school performance were hard to shake, much like your Year 1 decile also at 1.0, painting a picture of consistent challenges in keeping pace with peers. Even with your high family income offering some stability, it surprisingly nudged the outlook downward in this mix, as if resources alone couldn't fully bridge those academic hurdles. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 added another layer of concern, suggesting the transition to law's demands might be steeper for you. Factors like being white, planning full-time studies, and your gender mostly aligned with tougher odds in the model's view, though being female offered a small hopeful pull toward success. Together, these pieces wove a story of resilience needed but not yet fully proven, leading us to pause on your application to protect you from an uphill bar exam battle. It's not the end of your journey—many with similar starts have pivoted and soared by targeting those early academic footholds. What if you retake the LSAT or seek targeted tutoring to shift this narrative? You've got the drive; now it's about rewriting those key chapters. We're rooting for you.
